articles.title: Nigeria lifts tuition remittance cap even as visa concerns persist
articles.summary: The Central Bank of Nigeria has raised the maximum tuition fee remittance limit for students studying abroad from $15,000 to $25,000 per semester. Experts note that despite this increase, access to visas and adequate funding remain significant challenges for many prospective international students from Nigeria.
articles.insight:
This policy shift signals potential easing of financial constraints for Nigerian students pursuing overseas education, though its impact on actual enrolment remains uncertain given persistent visa hurdles and broader economic pressures in West Africa.  
Admissions teams should monitor uptake of the higher remittance cap alongside Nigerian visa refusal rates and local currency stability, while ensuring financial documentation guidance reflects the updated limit without assuming immediate demand growth.

articles.title: US visa processing centres in Africa to be reduced by more than half; only the latest barrier for African students
articles.summary: The US government plans to reduce the number of visa processing centres in Africa by more than half. This measure is part of a broader set of new policies aimed at making it more difficult and costly for students from many African countries to obtain US student visas.
articles.insight:
This news signals potential friction in US-bound African student pathways, possibly dampening demand or shifting preferences toward alternative destinations—though actual enrolment impact depends on how implementation interacts with existing visa backlogs and regional diplomatic relations.  
Admissions teams should monitor application volume and yield trends from key African countries over the next 6–12 months, while reviewing support services for visa documentation and exploring partnerships with local education agents to mitigate processing delays.

articles.title: Report: International students already studying in the UK or offshore through TNE represent an increasingly important recruitment opportunity
articles.summary: Tighter compliance thresholds for UK universities recruiting international students, including the Home Office’s “Red, Amber, Green” scheme, are now in effect. As of 1 June 2026, universities will be assessed under updated Basic Compliance Assessment (BCA) metrics, and only institutions rated green will meet the required standard.
articles.insight:
UK universities now face stricter compliance requirements for international student recruitment, with only “Green”-rated institutions permitted to sponsor visas from June 2026—potentially reshaping global student perceptions of UK study viability and indirectly influencing demand for alternative destinations like the US, Australia, and NZ.  
Admissions teams should monitor UK visa refusal trends, partner institution compliance ratings, and shifts in agent/student inquiries from key source markets—noting early signals of redirected applications but avoiding premature strategic pivots without corroborating data.
